text:
Let’s correct the record on gas prices.

At ~172.9¢/L in Ottawa, the actual government-added portion is about 39¢/L, broken down as:
	•	10¢/L federal excise tax
	•	9¢/L Ontario gas tax
	•	~20¢/L HST (yes, applied on top of those taxes — tax on tax)

That’s the visible part.

Then there are ‘invisible’ government costs:
	•	Federal Clean Fuel Regulations (estimated ~2–7¢/L depending on compliance)
	•	Ontario renewable fuel blending requirements (cost embedded, not itemized)

text:
Why??? Because we are taxed ontop of many hidden taxes.💪🏻💪🏻💪🏻
​These taxes are charged per litre, regardless of whether the price of oil goes up or down. They are usually included in the price you see on the billboard.

​**Federal Excise Tax (10.0¢/L): A flat rate that hasn't changed since 1995. It goes directly into federal general revenue.
  
​Federal Carbon Tax / Fuel Charge (~24.2¢/L): This is the "primary" carbon tax. As of April 1, 2026, the rate is $110 per tonne of CO_2, which adds roughly 24.2 cents to every litre of gasoline.

​Clean Fuel Regulations (CFR) (~7.0¢/L): Often called the "second carbon tax," this is a newer, more "hidden" cost. It requires refineries to reduce the carbon intensity of their fuel. These compliance costs are passed down to you and are estimated to add about 7 cents per litre in 2026. 

​Ontario Provincial Gas Tax (9.0¢/L): Ontario has maintained a reduced rate (down from the historical 14.7¢) to provide some relief at the pump. This 9-cent rate is currently the provincial standard.  
​
The "Visible" Tax: HST (13%)

​In Ontario, we pay 13% HST on gasoline. This is where the math gets frustrating for many drivers because it creates a "tax-on-tax" effect:  
​The gas station takes the base price of the fuel.
​They add the 50.2¢ of flat taxes mentioned above.
​The 13% HST is then calculated on that entire total.
​The Result: You are essentially paying a 13% tax on the carbon tax and the excise taxes.

text:
I have no idea who Aaron Gunn is, but this video is an insult to the human intellect. Since my background is in economics, let's debunk it.

1. First to answer the question in the title correctly. The reason why the national average gas price went from $1.25 to $1.80 at the exact moment the Iran war started is due to the closing of the straight of humus. This video completed avoids mentioning this fact, which is what's known as a  lie by omission. 

2. The price of oil is globally standardized, so Canada's oil reserves and/or oil production have no impact on gas prices. It's like claiming that Canadian electronics are expensive due to increased gold prices, so Canada needs to refine more gold to lower the price of electronics. Makes 0 sense. The phase "more expensive foreign oil" cracked me up as well, as it illustrates a total lack of understanding of how internationally traded commodities work.

3. Canada's oil production hit 4.2M barrels in 2025, which is an all-time high, largely due to the completion of the Trans Mountain Pipeline which which was purchased by the federal government in 2018 and completed in 2024, which by this guy's logic should mean gas prices should be at all-time lows, they are instead at all-time highs, due entirely to the Iran war.  

What's the actual solution? There isn't any. Cutting fuel taxes would not drop gas prices, it would only reduce your gas bill by 5%. Cutting the industrial carbon tax would only lower gas prices for industries, the actual price at gas stations would remain the same. The only thing that would directly and reliably lower gas prices is a profit margin cap, or a consumer gas subsidy, both of which are opposed by both Liberals and Conservatives, so gas prices will continue to stay high until the Iran oil shortage is over.

text:
Debunking the “The U.S. buys Canadian oil at a discount” myth:

The phrasing often suggests deliberate U.S. manipulation or exploitation - as if America is intentionally underpaying Canada. 

The price difference simply stems from market economics, crude quality variances, and infrastructure realities - not any rip-off.

Currently WCS heavy crude trades at roughly a $13–$14/bbl discount to lighter WTI. 

1. Crude quality and refining costs  
 
WCS is heavy and sour (high sulfur, thicker viscosity) — unlike light, sweet WTI, which is easier and cheaper to refine into high-value products like gasoline. 

Processing WCS requires extra steps: desulfurization, and coking that add costs.  
 
U.S. Gulf Coast refineries have invested billions in specialized equipment (cokers, hydrocrackers) to handle heavy sour crudes like WCS, Venezuela's, or Mexico's. 

The discount offsets those added expenses — it's economics, not charity. 

Buyers won't pay full light-crude prices for feedstock that costs more to turn into profit.

2. Limited market access & buyers
 
Most Canadian heavy oil is landlocked and piped south almost exclusively to U.S. refineries — the main (and best-equipped) market for it. 

Supply-and-demand basics apply: limited buyers mean lower prices.  
 
The U.S. has abundant domestic light crude plus alternatives (rising Venezuelan heavy volumes amid policy shifts), so it can choose not to buy if the price isn't attractive. 

Canada, lacking immediate global alternatives, operates in a constrained market. 

The fix is more export diversity, additional pipelines, refineries and tidewater access. 

3. Discount gap already narrowing:

The Trans Mountain Expansion has given West Coast access to Asian markets, boosting competition and trimming the discount by about $3/bbl on average in its early years. 

More routes = more buyers = stronger pricing via competition.

The bottom line:  
This is fair market pricing driven by quality, geography, transportation, & infrastructure - not U.S. exploitation. 

Diversifying Canadian exports is shrinking the discount naturally. 

Many U.S. figures, including Ambassador Pete Hoekstra on the Jasmin Laine podcast, have said they're “more than happy” to see Canada build that capacity and access other markets.

Feel free to to chime in with any points or nuances that may have been missed. 

The aim is to get to the truth with straightforward facts and no spin to get back to a strong North American partnership.

text: In regards to your absolutely correct comments about the exchange rate between the CDN and US dollars I want to add something important you haven't mentioned. Canada always judges the 'value' of it's dollar by comparing it to the US dollar. Historically, it is not that unusual for the CDN dollar to be valued in the .70's and .80's cent range compared to the US dollar. However, the US dollar has also lost 25% of it's purchasing power since 2015. Therefore, our CDN dollar at .72 means it is worth MUCH less now than it was in the past when the US dollar was healthier. Our CDN dollar is worth only 72% of a very weak US dollar. Everything is now so expensive in Canada because of these reasons. Much of what Canadians eat and otherwise consume still comes from the USA. Elbows up has meant absolutely nothing. We cannot escape the fact that Canada is extremely reliant on the US for economic survival and it always will be.

text: Canada doesn't "have" its own oil. The oil is physically in the ground in Canada, but it's not Canada's. It belongs to the for-profit companies that extract it, until they sell it to refineries. They sell it to the highest bidder in order to maximize profit. After it's refined, the refineries sell their products to the highest bidder in order to maximize profit. When refineries that normally get oil from the Persian Gulf can't anymore, they start bidding for oil from other places, and the price goes up. Producers see what desperate refineries are willing to pay, so that's what they charge to everyone.
